questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Helmholtz equation ∇²ψ + k²ψ = 0 arises from the separation of which equation?
questions.solution: Starting from the wave equation ∇²ψ − (1/c²)∂²ψ/∂t² = 0 and assuming ψ(r,t) = ψ(r)e^(−iωt), substitution yields ∇²ψ + (ω²/c²)ψ = 0, i.e. the Helmholtz equation with k = ω/c. Laplace's equation is the static limit (k = 0).

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Two solutions of y'' + P(x)y' + Q(x)y = 0 have Wronskian W(x). If the solutions are linearly dependent, W(x) equals:
questions.solution: By Abel's identity, W(x) = C·e^(−∫P dx). Two solutions are linearly dependent if and only if W(x) = 0 for all x. This is the fundamental criterion for linear independence of solutions of second-order ODEs.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: If A and B are similar matrices (B = P⁻¹AP), which property is NOT necessarily preserved?
questions.solution: Similar matrices share eigenvalues, determinant, trace, rank, and characteristic polynomial. However, eigenvectors are NOT preserved: if Ax = λx, then B(P⁻¹x) = λ(P⁻¹x), so eigenvectors of B are P⁻¹ times those of A.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Which Fourier transform property states that convolution in time corresponds to multiplication in frequency?
questions.solution: The convolution theorem: F{f * g} = F{f}·F{g}. Time-shifting gives F{f(t−t₀)} = e^(−iωt₀)F(ω). Frequency-shifting gives F{e^(iω₀t)f(t)} = F(ω−ω₀). Parseval's theorem relates energy in time and frequency domains.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: A solid sphere (I = 2MR²/5) rolls without slipping down an incline of angle θ. Its centre-of-mass acceleration is:
questions.solution: ma = Mg sin θ − f and τ = fR = Iα with a = Rα. So f = (2/5)Ma. Substituting: Ma = Mg sin θ − (2/5)Ma → a = (5/7)g sin θ. The factor 5/7 accounts for rotational KE.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In Kaluza-Klein theory, electromagnetism is unified with gravity by compactifying general relativity on:
questions.solution: Kaluza (1921) and Klein (1926) showed that 5D general relativity with the 5th dimension compactified on a circle of radius R reproduces 4D gravity plus electromagnetism. The off-diagonal g_μ5 components become the electromagnetic potential A_μ.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: For an integrable Hamiltonian system with action-angle variables (J, θ), the Hamiltonian depends only on:
questions.solution: For integrable systems, H = H(J₁,...,Jₙ) depends only on action variables. The angles evolve linearly: θᵢ(t) = θᵢ(0) + ωᵢt where ωᵢ = ∂H/∂Jᵢ, making analysis of periodic/quasi-periodic motion straightforward.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Routhian R is formed by a partial Legendre transform over cyclic coordinates. It is defined as:
questions.solution: The Routhian performs a partial Legendre transform: R = Σ_(cyclic) pᵢq̇ᵢ − L, reducing degrees of freedom by treating cyclic momenta as parameters. It combines Lagrangian and Hamiltonian approaches for systems with mixed cyclic and non-cyclic coordinates.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The normal modes of a string of length L fixed at both ends have frequencies:
questions.solution: Boundary conditions give kₙ = nπ/L. Since ω = vk and f = ω/(2π), fₙ = nv/(2L) = nf₁. Integer multiples of the fundamental give harmonic overtones.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: A point charge q is at distance d from the centre of a grounded conducting sphere of radius R. The image charge magnitude is:
questions.solution: By the method of images, q' = −q(R/d) placed at distance b = R²/d from the centre (inside sphere) satisfies φ = 0 on the sphere surface.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In a symmetric dielectric slab waveguide (core n₁, cladding n₂), guided modes exist when β satisfies:
questions.solution: Guided modes require kn₂ < β < kn₁ (k = ω/c). This ensures oscillatory fields in the core and evanescent fields in the cladding. The cutoff is determined by the V-number V = kd√(n₁²−n₂²)/2.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Faraday rotation angle for light propagating through a medium in a magnetic field is proportional to:
questions.solution: The Faraday rotation θ = VBL, where V is the Verdet constant, B is the field component along propagation, and L is the path length. This linear dependence on both B and L distinguishes it from the Cotton-Mouton effect.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In second harmonic generation (SHG), a laser beam of frequency ω produces output at frequency:
questions.solution: In SHG, two photons of frequency ω combine in a χ⁽²⁾ nonlinear medium to produce one photon at 2ω. Phase matching k₂ω = 2kω is required. KDP and BBO crystals convert 1064 nm Nd:YAG light to 532 nm green.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Resonant inductive coupling efficiency depends most critically on:
options: The figure of merit k²Q₁Q₂ (coupling × quality factors)
questions.solution: Efficiency η ≈ k²Q₁Q₂/(1+√(1+k²Q₁Q₂))². The figure of merit k²Q₁Q₂ (k = coupling coefficient, Q = quality factor) determines maximum achievable efficiency. Higher k and Q improve efficiency.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The ground state energy of the quantum harmonic oscillator is:
questions.solution: Eₙ = (n+½)ℏω. The ground state (n=0) has E₀ = ½ℏω — the zero-point energy arising from the uncertainty principle. It has measurable consequences (e.g., liquid helium not freezing at 1 atm).

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: A charged particle in uniform magnetic field B has Landau levels with energy spacing equal to:
questions.solution: Landau levels: Eₙ = ℏωc(n+½) where ωc = eB/m is the cyclotron frequency. The spacing is ℏωc = ℏeB/m. This quantisation of cyclotron orbits is the basis for the quantum Hall effect.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In the Aharonov-Bohm effect, the phase shift between electron paths around a solenoid enclosing flux Φ is:
questions.solution: The AB phase shift is Δφ = (e/ℏ)∮A·dl = eΦ/ℏ = 2π(Φ/Φ₀) where Φ₀ = h/e is the flux quantum. This demonstrates that the vector potential A has physical significance even where B = 0.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Büttiker-Landauer traversal time for quantum tunnelling through a barrier is approximately:
questions.solution: The Büttiker-Landauer time τ ≈ m/(ℏκ²) = ℏ/(2(V₀−E)) for a rectangular barrier. Tunnelling is not instantaneous — the traversal time depends on barrier height and width through the decay constant κ.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Quantum decoherence causes the transition from quantum to classical behaviour by:
options: Decaying off-diagonal density matrix elements via entanglement with environment
Converting all superpositions to mixed states at T = 0
questions.solution: Decoherence causes off-diagonal elements of the reduced density matrix to decay (T₂ relaxation), suppressing quantum interference. It does NOT collapse the wavefunction — it creates entanglement with the environment, making interference unobservable.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Boltzmann entropy S = k_B ln Ω relates entropy to:
options: Number of accessible microstates Ω at fixed E, V, N
questions.solution: S = k_B ln Ω for the microcanonical ensemble, where Ω(E,V,N) counts microstates compatible with macroscopic constraints. Temperature is derived: 1/T = ∂S/∂E.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In the canonical ensemble, the relative energy fluctuation ⟨(ΔE)²⟩/⟨E⟩² for an ideal gas of N particles scales as:
questions.solution: ⟨(ΔE)²⟩ = k_BT²C_V. For ideal gas: C_V = (3/2)Nk_B, ⟨E⟩ = (3/2)Nk_BT. So ⟨(ΔE)²⟩/⟨E⟩² = 1/N. Fluctuations become negligible for macroscopic N.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The lambda point (Tλ ≈ 2.17 K) in liquid ⁴He marks the transition to:
options: A pure Bose-Einstein condensate of all atoms
The superfluid He-II phase with zero viscosity and quantised vortices
questions.solution: Below Tλ, liquid ⁴He enters He-II: a superfluid with zero viscosity, infinite thermal conductivity, and quantised circulation. Related to but not identical to BEC — interactions modify the picture. The specific heat shows a characteristic lambda-shaped divergence.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Shannon entropy H = −Σ pᵢ log₂ pᵢ is maximised when:
questions.solution: Shannon entropy is maximised for uniform distribution (pᵢ = 1/n), giving H = log₂ n — maximum uncertainty. This connects to Boltzmann entropy: S = k_B ln W for equal-probability microstates.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Pauli exclusion principle states no two identical fermions can share the same:
questions.solution: No two identical fermions can have the same complete set of quantum numbers. Two electrons CAN share an orbital (same n, l, mₗ) but must have opposite spins (mₛ = ±½).

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Larmor precession frequency ωL of a magnetic moment in field B is:
questions.solution: ωL = gμBB/ℏ = γB (gyromagnetic ratio γ = gμB/ℏ). The torque τ = μ×B causes angular momentum to precess around B. Basis of NMR, ESR, and atomic magnetometry.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The intensity of a vibronic transition in the Franck-Condon principle is proportional to:
questions.solution: The Franck-Condon factor |⟨v'|v''⟩|² determines vibronic line intensity. The electronic transition is fast (vertical), so vibrational wavefunctions overlap at the same internuclear distance. Maximum overlap gives the most intense line.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.topic: Atomic & Molecular Physics · Molecular Term Symbols
questions.stem: The ground state molecular term symbol of O₂ is:
questions.solution: O₂ has two unpaired electrons in π* antibonding orbitals. By Hund's rules, maximum spin: S=1 (triplet), giving ³Σ_g⁻. This paramagnetic triplet explains why liquid O₂ is attracted to magnets.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Penning ionization (A* + B → A + B⁺ + e⁻) occurs when:
options: The excitation energy of A* exceeds the ionisation energy of B
Both atoms are in the ground state
A* has a shorter lifetime than the collision time
B has higher ionisation energy than A
questions.solution: Energetically allowed when E*(A) > IE(B). Metastable He* (19.8 eV) can ionise most atoms/molecules, making it useful in ionisation detectors and plasma sources.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Debye-Waller factor e^(−2W) describes reduction of X-ray diffraction intensity due to:
options: Thermal vibrations of atoms about lattice positions
questions.solution: e^(−2W) accounts for Bragg peak attenuation from thermal motion. 2W = ⟨(Δu·Q)²⟩ where Δu is atomic displacement and Q is scattering vector. At high T, W ∝ T.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In Compton scattering, the wavelength shift Δλ depends on:
options: Scattering angle θ: Δλ = (h/mₑc)(1 − cos θ)
questions.solution: Δλ = λ_C(1 − cos θ) where λ_C = h/mₑc ≈ 2.43 pm. The shift depends only on angle, confirming photon-electron collisions are elastic and particle-like.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In semiconductor cyclotron resonance, the effective mass m* is determined from resonance frequency ωc and field B as:
questions.solution: ωc = eB/m*, so m* = eB/ωc. Measuring microwave absorption at resonance for known frequency gives effective mass — crucial for mapping semiconductor band structures.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: In a GaAs/AlGaAs quantum well, electrons are confined in GaAs because:
options: Conduction band offset creates a potential well in the lower-bandgap GaAs
questions.solution: GaAs bandgap (~1.42 eV) < AlGaAs (~1.8 eV). The conduction band offset confines electrons in GaAs, creating a 2D electron gas with very high mobility — the basis of HEMTs and quantum Hall effect.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Thermal noise voltage in a resistor R at temperature T over bandwidth Δf is:
questions.solution: Johnson-Nyquist noise: ⟨V²⟩ = 4kTRΔf. White noise independent of frequency, setting the fundamental noise floor for all electronic circuits.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The Nyquist-Shannon theorem requires sampling at rate at least:
questions.solution: Nyquist rate = 2f_max. Sampling below this causes aliasing. For audio (20 kHz), standard CD rate 44.1 kHz > 40 kHz.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: For an 8-bit R-2R DAC with Vref = 5V and input = 0b10000000 (MSB only), the output is:
questions.solution: Input value = 128. Vout = Vref × (input/2⁸) = 5 × (128/256) = 2.50 V. The MSB contributes exactly Vref/2.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The basic programmable element inside an FPGA is a:
questions.solution: FPGA CLBs contain LUTs (implementing any Boolean function via truth table in SRAM) and flip-flops (for sequential logic). A k-input LUT handles any k-variable Boolean function.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: An OTA differs from a standard op-amp in that its output is:
options: Current proportional to differential input (transconductance gₘ)
questions.solution: OTA: Iout = gₘ(V+ − V⁻), a voltage-controlled current source. Unlike standard op-amps (voltage output), OTAs output current. gₘ is controllable via bias current — useful for variable-gain amplifiers, filters, and analog multipliers.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: Nuclear isomers (e.g. ⁹⁹ᵐTc) are long-lived because:
options: They have different charge than the ground state
Gamma decay requires large ΔJ, making it highly forbidden
They are held by strong force
questions.solution: Isomers are metastable excited states with large spin difference ΔJ from ground state. The large angular momentum change makes gamma decay highly forbidden, giving long half-lives (ns to years). ⁹⁹ᵐTc (t₁/₂ = 6h) is widely used in medical imaging.

questions.subject: Domain Knowledge
questions.stem: The strong nuclear force (QCD) exhibits confinement, meaning:
options: Quarks cannot exist as free particles
It acts only on charged particles
It is weaker than EM at all distances
questions.solution: QCD confinement: quarks and gluons are never observed isolated. The interquark potential is弱 at short distances (asymptotic freedom) but grows linearly with separation, preventing liberation.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A completes a task in 15 days, B in 20 days. Working together, they finish in:
questions.solution: Combined rate = 1/15 + 1/20 = 7/60 per day. Days = 60/7 ≈ 8.57.

questions.stem: Average of five numbers is 42. Excluding one number, average becomes 38. The excluded number is:

questions.stem: If A:B = 2:3 and B:C = 4:5, then A:B:C =?

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A train 150 m long passes a pole in 15 sec. Time to pass a 200 m platform?
questions.solution: Speed = 150/15 = 10 m/s. Total distance = 150 + 200 = 350 m. Time = 350/10 = 35 sec.

questions.stem: A boat goes 24 km upstream in 6 h and 24 km downstream in 3 h. Speed of current?
questions.solution: Upstream = 24/6 = 4 km/h. Downstream = 24/3 = 8 km/h. Current = (8−4)/2 = 2 km/h.

questions.stem: Pipe A fills in 12 h, Pipe B empties in 18 h. Both open, tank full in:
questions.solution: Net rate = 1/12 − 1/18 = 1/36 per hour. Time = 36 hours.

questions.stem: Water must be mixed with milk at Rs.40/litre to get mixture at Rs.25/litre in ratio:

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Two trains of 120 m and 180 m run in opposite directions at 54 and 72 km/h. Time to cross?
questions.solution: Relative speed = 54+72 = 126 km/h = 35 m/s. Distance = 300 m. Time = 300/35 ≈ 8.57 sec. Closest: 8 sec.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: If DELHI is coded as FGNK, then MUMBAI is coded as:
questions.solution: Each letter shifted by +2: D→F, E→G, L→N, H→J, I→K. So DELHI → FGNJK... Let me verify: D(4)→F(6)+2, E(5)→G(7)+2, L(12)→N(14)+2, H(8)→J(10)+2, I(9)→K(11)+2. DELHI → FGNJK. For MUMBAI: M(13)→O(15), U(21)→W(23), M(13)→O(15), B(2)→D(4), A(1)→C(3), I(9)→K(11) = OWODCK.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A says to B, 'Your mother is the only daughter of my mother.' How is A related to B?
questions.solution: 'Only daughter of my mother' = A herself. So 'your mother' = A. A is B's mother.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: A man walks 8 km North, turns right walks 6 km, turns right walks 8 km. How far from start and direction?
questions.solution: Start: 8 km N → (0,8). Right(E) 6 km → (6,8). Right(S) 8 km → (6,0). Distance = 6 km East.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Statements: All dogs are animals. Some animals are cats. Conclusions: I. Some dogs are cats. II. Some cats are animals.
questions.solution: From the given statements, 'Some cats are animals' follows by conversion of statement 2. We cannot conclude 'Some dogs are cats' — the cats that are animals may not be dogs.

questions.stem: If X > Y ≥ Z > W, which is definitely true?
questions.solution: X > Z (via Y), X > W (transitivity), Y > W (direct). All are true.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: Five persons A, B, C, D, E sit in a row facing north. C is to the immediate right of A. B is at one end. E is to the immediate left of D. D is not next to B. Who sits at the left end?
questions.solution: B at right end (not next to D). E left of D together. C right of A. Arrangement: E D A C B. Left end = E.

questions.subject: Aptitude
questions.stem: In a class of 40, Rahul's rank from top is 15. Rank from bottom?
questions.solution: Rank from bottom = 40 − 15 + 1 = 26.

questions.stem: Four friends P, Q, R, S sit around a square table, one on each side. P is to the left of Q. R is opposite P. Who is to the right of R?
questions.solution: P left of Q (clockwise: Q, P, S, R). R opposite P. S is to the right of R.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: India's coal production target for FY2025 is:
questions.solution: India targets 1200 MT of coal production.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: India's first bullet train project connects which two cities?
questions.solution: The first bullet train project connects Mumbai and Ahmedabad.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: BPCL recently commissioned a new refinery in:
questions.solution: BPCL commissioned a refinery in Andhra Pradesh.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: BRICS expanded to include which new members in 2024?
questions.solution: Saudi Arabia, Iran, and UAE joined BRICS.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: The G20 summit under India's presidency was held in:
questions.solution: The G20 summit was held in New Delhi.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: The Ujjwala Yojana provides:
questions.solution: Ujjwala Yojana provides free LPG connections to BPL families.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: The President of India is elected by:
questions.solution: The President is elected by an electoral college.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: Upper House of Indian Parliament:
questions.solution: Rajya Sabha is the Upper House.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: How many fundamental rights are currently recognized?
questions.solution: There are 6 fundamental rights currently recognized.

questions.subject: General Awareness
questions.stem: Minimum age for Lok Sabha membership:
questions.solution: Minimum age for Lok Sabha membership is 25 years.

questions.subject: English
questions.stem: What is the synonym of 'ephemeral'?
questions.solution: Ephemeral means short-lived or transient.

questions.subject: English
questions.stem: She is proficient ___ English.
questions.solution: Proficient + in is the correct collocation.

questions.subject: English
questions.stem: The teacher told the students ___ quietly.
questions.solution: Infinitive after 'told': told someone to do something.

questions.subject: English
questions.stem: What is the meaning of 'pragmatic'?
questions.solution: Pragmatic means dealing with things practically.

questions.subject: English
questions.stem: ___ honest man always speaks the truth.
questions.solution: 'An' before words starting with silent 'h'.

questions.subject: English
questions.stem: The meeting was put ___ Friday.
questions.solution: Put off means to postpone.

questions.subject: English
questions.stem: The manager, along with his team, ___ present at the meeting.
questions.solution: When 'along with' is used, the verb agrees with the first subject (manager → was).

questions.subject: English
questions.stem: What is the meaning of 'diligent'?
questions.solution: Diligent means showing care and effort in one's work.

questions.subject: English
questions.stem: Choose the antonym of 'benevolent':
questions.solution: Benevolent means well-meaning; malevolent means ill-willed.

questions.subject: English
questions.stem: By the time we arrive, they ___ already left.
questions.solution: Future perfect: will have + past participle.
